rulings.Case Title: CONSERVATORSHIP OF DONALD M. WILSON
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Ex Parte Application For Right To Attach Order/Writ Of Attachment
rulings.Rulings: Daniel Cunningham's Application for Right to Attach Order and Writ of Attachment is GRANTED as to all Respondents. Based on admissible evidence, the Court finds that the Conservator has satisfied his burden as the party moving for relief under CCP § 484.090. As to Eric and Rebecca Dinaburg (collectively, "the Dinaburgs"), Gilles Attia, and the Zalgud Company, Inc., the Conservator has submitted sufficient evidence to show that (1) his claim for financial elder abuse against all Respondents is a claim upon which attachment may be issued, "whether or not other forms of relief are requested" (Welf. & Inst. Code § 15657.01); (2) that his elder abuse claim has "probable validity"; (3) the Application is not sought for a purpose "other than the recovery on the claim upon which the attachment is based"; and, (4) the amount sought to be secured is greater than zero. As to the Dinaburgs, their failure to file an opposition is conclusive.(CCP § 484.060(a).) As to Attia and the Zalgud Co., the Conservator has demonstrated the probable validity of his claim for financial elder abuse against those respondents as there is sufficient evidence to support a finding that they "took, secreted, and obtained" Mr. Wilson's property for a "wrongful use" and/or with the intent to defraud. Attia and Zalgud are responsible as principals, having directly participated in the financial elder abuse both by drafting and taking possession of the property via the "Rental" and "Purchase" agreements, notwithstanding the subsequent transfer of the sale proceeds to the Dinaburgs. At a minimum, Attia and Zalgud assisted in elder abuse as they "knew or should have known" that the transaction(s) at issue would be "likely to be harmful" to Mr. Wilson. The Court finds that the evidentiary standard regarding "probable validity" of the elder abuse claim is met even without reference to Probate Code § 21380(c), but sees no reason why that section and the corresponding analysis isn't also helpful in determining whether fraud and/or undue influence occurred. The Court further finds that the Conservator has shown "probable validity" with respect to his "donative transfer" claims. (Probate Code § 21380; Jenkins v. Teegarden (2014) 230 Cal.App.4th 1128.) Under CCP § 484.090(b), if the court finds that "the defendant has failed to prove that all the property sought to be attached is exempt from attachment, it shall order a writ of attachment to be issued upon the filing of an undertaking¿" Attia and Zalgud have not taken the opportunity to present evidence that any of the properties sought to be attached are exempt under the applicable code sections. (CCP § 484.070(a).) Therefore, the Conservator's request for a right to attach order in the amount of $1,681,667 is GRANTED, a number that takes into account $200,000 in estimated reasonable attorney fees and costs, and the $1,481,667 profit obtained after the sale of 835 Sanchez Street. A writ of attachment shall issue as to all Respondents and all property identified in the Application (except the vague term "securities") upon the filing of a $10,000 undertaking, as no party has objected to the presumed statutory amount of the undertaking. (CCP section 489.220.) Any party wishing to contest this tentative ruling must so notify the opposing parties and the courtroom clerk (415-551-3702) no later than 4 p.m. on Tuesday, December 11, 2018.

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE RICHARD E. SIMMONS 2013 REVOCABLE LIVING TRUST
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To File Records Under Seal
rulings.Rulings: Petitioner Helen D'Vorah Simmons' Motion to File Records Under Seal is DENIED without prejudice. The burden is on the party moving to have records sealed to set forth specific facts in support of the motion. (H.B. Fuller Co. v. Doe, 151 Cal.App.4th 879, 895-896 [statements that are too conclusory, abstract or generic are not sufficient to satisfy this burden].) Not only has Petitioner failed to apply the factors set forth in Rule 2.550(d), she has failed to set forth any remotely "specific facts" in support of her request to seal. Further, it is unclear why the entirety of the Petition for Instructions for Approval of Settlement Agreement and to Terminate Trust, and the entirety of the Settlement Agreement attached thereto, need to be sealed. Under Rule 2.550(d)(4)-(d)(5), the proposed sealing must be "narrowly tailored" and "no less restrictive means" must be available to achieve the overriding interest. (See Huffy Corp. v. Sup. Ct. (2003)112 Cal.App.4th 97, 107 ["Defendant has not asked to seal any particular parts of the settlement agreement. Much of the settlement agreement is routine verbiage which appears in most settlement agreements."]; see also Universal City Studios, Inc. v. Sup.Ct. (2003) 110 Cal.App.4th 1273, 1284.) Any party wishing to contest this tentative ruling must so notify the courtroom clerk (415-551-3702) and the other parties no later than 4 p.m. on Tuesday, December 11, 2018.

[CRC 7.53(a).] Notice of the amended petition must be mailed and published for the 1/9/19 hearing date. (An amended petition supercedes a "supplement" that might have provided the same information.)
